name: Miners' Way Loop
name_original: Subida al Castillo de Chillón — circular desde Almadén
description: Jog this moderate 6.3-mile circular route from Almadén to Castillo de Chillón, enjoying historical mining landscapes and panoramic views.
region_intro: Jogging routes in Ciudad Real offer a diverse landscape for runners, blending urban green spaces with expansive natural parks and varied terrain. The region features well-maintained paths through city parks, serene wetland trails, and more challenging mountain routes. Runners can explore areas characterized by Mediterranean flora, unique inland wetlands, and rolling hills, providing options for various fitness levels. Best jogging routes in Ciudad Real The most popular jogging route is Vía Verde de Poblete — round trip, a 2.8 miles (4.5 km) trail that takes 29 minutes to complete. This accessible greenway offers a pleasant, flat surface ideal for a leisurely run. Another top favourite among local runners is Camino Manchego de Santiago and La Atalaya Park Loop, a moderate 8.5 miles (13.8 km) path. This route provides panoramic views from La Atalaya Park and includes varied terrain. Local runners also love the Cascada de la Batanera de Sierra Madrona — circular por las Pinturas rupestres Peña escrita, a 4.9 miles (7.9 km) trail leading through natural landscapes with historical rock paintings, often completed in about 55 minutes. Jogging in Ciudad Real is defined by urban green spaces, unique wetlands, and varied mountain and hill trails. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy city loops to more challenging ascents. The routes in Ciudad Real are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 70 reviews. More than 3000 runners have used komoot to explore Ciudad Real's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Ciudad Real?
A: Ciudad Real offers a wide variety of running routes, with over 460 options available on komoot. These range from easy urban jogs to more challenging mountain trails, catering to all fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging routes in Ciudad Real?
A: You'll find a diverse range of terrains. The region features well-maintained paths through city parks like Gasset Park, serene wetland trails in areas such as Tablas de Daimiel National Park, and more challenging mountain routes in Sierra Madrona. Expect a mix of flat greenways, riverside paths, and hilly ascents with panoramic views.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Ciudad Real has several easy and family-friendly running routes. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1210330588/via-verde-de-poblete-round-trip" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde de Poblete — round trip&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 4.5 km accessible greenway with a pleasant, flat surface. Another easy circular route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1211472556/laguna-del-prado-circular-by-pozuelo-de-calatrava" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Prado — circular by Pozuelo de Calatrava&lt;/a&gt;, offering tranquil waterside views.

Q: Can I find challenging running trails with significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. For experienced runners seeking a challenge, the province offers ascents to peaks like Riscos del Amor and La Bañuela in Sierra Madrona. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1210094153/cerro-de-san-agustin-by-the-laguna-de-almodovar-circular-from-puertollano" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Agustín by the Laguna de Almodóvar&lt;/a&gt; route is a difficult 15.5 km trail with over 230 meters of elevation gain, providing a good workout and rewarding views.

Q: Are there running routes that feature water or lakes?
A: Yes, Ciudad Real is home to several beautiful routes near water. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1210109854/natural-path-of-the-guadiana-through-the-el-vicario-reservoir-circular-through-peralvillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural path of the Guadiana through the El Vicario Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers a scenic 10.5 km circular run along the water. You can also explore the unique inland wetlands of Tablas de Daimiel National Park, which features wooden walkways over serene waters, perfect for a peaceful run.

Q: What are some running routes with historical or cultural points of interest?
A: For a blend of nature and history, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1304449386/cascada-de-la-batanera-de-sierra-madrona-circular-por-las-pinturas-rupestres-pena-escrita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada de la Batanera de Sierra Madrona&lt;/a&gt;. This 7.9 km circular trail includes prehistoric rock paintings, offering a unique cultural experience. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3882572" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alarcos Archaeological Park&lt;/a&gt; is a significant historical site near some running paths.

Q: Can I find running routes with panoramic views?
A: Definitely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1209250698/camino-manchego-de-santiago-and-la-atalaya-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Manchego de Santiago and La Atalaya Park Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 13.8 km route that provides excellent panoramic views from La Atalaya Park. The mountainous regions, such as Sierra Madrona, also offer challenging trails with breathtaking vistas.

Q: Are there any running routes where I might spot wildlife?
A: Yes, several natural areas are excellent for wildlife spotting while running. Cabañeros National Park, with its riverbanks and Mediterranean flora, is known for its diverse fauna. Tablas de Daimiel National Park, a unique wetland, is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve and a prime location for birdwatching. The Ribera del Embalse de El Vicario also has strategic viewpoints for observing birds.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Ciudad Real?
A: The running routes in Ciudad Real are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 70 reviews. Runners often praise the region's diverse landscapes, from tranquil wetland paths to challenging mountain trails, and the accessibility of urban green spaces like Gasset Park.

Q: Are there any running routes that include waterfalls?
A: Yes, the Ruidera Lakes Natural Park, about an hour from Ciudad Real, features a chain of turquoise lagoons and cascading waterfalls. While not directly in Ciudad Real city, it's a fantastic regional option for a scenic run. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1304449386/cascada-de-la-batanera-de-sierra-madrona-circular-por-las-pinturas-rupestres-pena-escrita" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cascada de la Batanera de Sierra Madrona&lt;/a&gt; also offers a moderate circular trail that includes a waterfall.

Q: When is the best time of year to go running in Ciudad Real?
A: Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for running in Ciudad Real, with mild temperatures and less extreme weather. Summers can be very hot, especially midday, so early morning or late evening runs are recommended. Winters are typically mild, making running enjoyable year-round, though some higher elevation trails might be cooler.

Q: Are there urban green spaces for running within Ciudad Real city?
A: Absolutely. Gasset Park is the largest green space within Ciudad Real, offering beautiful areas with fountains, sculptures, and trees, perfect for a leisurely jog. The Prado Gardens, near the cathedral, also provide a relaxing environment for a light run amidst urban sculptures.
tips: Castle that is located right at the top of the town of Almadén and that is also called the Ermita Virgen del Castillo. The mountain has the same name as the castle.

In the Alcudia Valley lies Almadén, a town linked to mining since ancient times, dyed with the reddish cinnabar of its cave paintings. The Romans opened up this land to extract the precious metal coveted in the empire as a dye, cosmetic or ointment. Since those times and despite the vagaries of exploitation or demand, Almadén has produced a third of the mercury consumed by humanity. 
The Almadén Mining Park offers us a trip to the interior of one of the oldest mines in the world. The park is completed with museums, interpretation centres and interesting displays of industrial archaeology. The Old School of Mine Foremen, created in the 18th century for the Study of Subterranean Geometry and Mineralogy. The House of the Superintendence located next to the mine allowed for greater control of it, of Baroque style only the enclosure remains. The San Rafael Miners' Hospital houses the historical archive of the Almadén Mines.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog this route?
faqs.answer: For a jogger, this route is estimated to take approximately 1 hour and 16 minutes to complete. Keep in mind that this is an estimate, and your actual time may vary based on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: While the trail is classified as 'moderate' and involves significant elevation changes, it can be challenging for absolute beginners. Some sections may be unpaved or uneven, requiring good fitness and sure-footedness. If you're a beginner, consider a slower pace or walking some of the steeper sections.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The trail is a circular route starting from Almadén. While specific parking details aren't provided, as a circular route from Almadén, you would typically find parking within the town or near the designated trailhead. Look for public parking areas in Almadén.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for jogging on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied. As a 'moderate' trail, expect a mix of accessible paths and sections that may be unpaved, uneven, or rocky. You'll encounter ascents and descents, so be prepared for a workout that goes beyond a casual flat run, potentially involving brisk uphill walking and careful downhill navigation.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a unique blend of natural landscapes and historical significance. You'll pass through the Montes Sur region of Chillón and the Parque Minero de Almadén, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Expect impressive panoramic views from the Virgen del Castillo chapel, potentially spanning Castilla-La Mancha, Extremadura, and Andalusia, surrounded by the Sierra de los Canalizos and Sierras de Almadén-Chillón-Guadalmez mountain ranges.

faqs.question: What historical sites or landmarks will I see on the trail?
faqs.answer: A major highlight of this route is the Castillo de Chillón, a historical castle. The trail also takes you through the Parque Minero de Almadén, known for its historic mercury mines, offering a distinctive backdrop of industrial heritage alongside nature.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Camino de los Mineros - Etapa 1, Calle de la bajada al Pozuelo, Camino de Chillón, Calle Casas Florido, and Escombrera de la Mina.

faqs.question: Is Ascent to the Castillo de Chillón — circular from Almadén dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The information available does not explicitly state restrictions on dogs. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in protected natural areas like the Sierra de los Canalizos and Sierras de Almadén-Chillón-Guadalmez, and when passing through historical sites or populated areas. Please respect local regulations and wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail or the Parque Minero de Almadén?
faqs.answer: The trail itself, being an outdoor route, generally does not require a permit or entrance fee. However, since the route passes through the Parque Minero de Almadén, which is a UNESCO World Heritage site, there might be specific regulations or entrance fees for accessing certain facilities or exhibitions within the mining park itself, separate from the trail. It's advisable to check the official website for the Parque Minero de Almadén for the latest information on visitor access and fees.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: Given the region's climate, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally the most pleasant times for jogging, offering milder temperatures. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late evening runs preferable if visiting during those months. Winters are typically mild but can be damp.

faqs.question: What should I bring for jogging this route?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, it's advisable to bring appropriate jogging shoes with good grip, sufficient water, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for the weather. A small backpack for essentials and a fully charged phone are also recommended.
